content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=2 x^3+\mathrm{m} x^2-13 x+\mathrm{n}$ and 2,3 are the roots of the equation $\mathrm{f}(x)=0$ then the value of $4 m+5 n$ is
explanation:
$$ f(x)=2 x^3+m x^2-13 x+n $$

2,3 are the roots of the given equation $\mathrm{f}(x)=0$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(2)^3+\mathrm{m}(2)^2-13(2)+\mathrm{n}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 16+4 \mathrm{~m}-26+\mathrm{n}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{n}=10 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Also, $\mathrm{f}(3)=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2(3)^3+\mathrm{m}(3)^2-13(3)+\mathrm{n}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 54+9 \mathrm{~m}-39+\mathrm{n}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 9 \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{n}=-15 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Subtracting (i) from (ii), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 5 \mathrm{~m}=-25 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}=-5 \end{aligned} $$

Substituting $m=-5$ in equation (i), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4(-5)+n=10 \\ & \Rightarrow n=10+20 \\ \Rightarrow & n=30 \\ \therefore \quad & 4 m+5 n=4(-5)+5(30) \\ & \quad=-20+150=130 \end{aligned} $$

content: The value of 'a' so that the sum of squares of the roots of the equation $x^2-(a-2) x-a+1=0$ assumes the least value is
explanation:
Given, $x^2-(a-2) x-a+1=0$

Let the roots of the given equation be $\alpha$ and $\beta$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & \alpha+\beta=\frac{-(-(a-2))}{1} \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=a-2 \\ & \text { and } \alpha \beta=\frac{-a+1}{1}=-a+1 \end{aligned} $$

Sum of squares of the roots,

$$ \begin{aligned} \alpha^2+\beta^2 & =(\alpha+\beta)^2-2 \alpha \beta \\ & =(a-2)^2-2(-a+1) \\ & =(a-2)^2+2 a-2 \\ & =a^2-4 a+4+2 a-2 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \alpha^2+\beta^2=a^2-2 a+2\\ &\text { Quadratic form } a x^2+b x+c \text { is min / max at }\\ &\begin{aligned} & x=\frac{-b}{2 a} \\ \therefore \quad & a=-\frac{(-2)}{2 \times 1}=1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$e^{\sin x}-e^{-\sin x}-4=0$$

Let $\mathrm{e}^{\sin x}=y$, then the given equation can be written as $y^2-4 y-1=0 \Rightarrow y=2 \pm \sqrt{5}$

But the value of $y=\mathrm{e}^{\sin x}$ is always positive, so

$$\begin{array}{ll} y=2+\sqrt{5} & \ldots[\because 2<\sqrt{5}] \\ \Rightarrow e^{\sin x}=2+\sqrt{5} & \end{array}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\sin x}>\mathrm{e} & \ldots[\because 2+\sqrt{5}>\mathrm{e}] \\ \Rightarrow \sin x>1 \end{array}$$

which is not possible, since $\sin x$ cannot be greater than 1.

Hence, no real value of $x$ satisfies the given equation.

content: Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the roots of the equation $x^2-\mathrm{p} x+\mathrm{r}=0$ and $\frac{\alpha}{2}, 2 \beta$ be the roots of the equation $x^2-q x+r=0$. Then the value of r is
explanation:
$\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of $x^2-\mathrm{p} x+\mathrm{r}=0$

$\therefore \quad$ sum of roots $=\alpha+\beta$

$$\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=p\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

$\frac{\alpha}{2}, 2 \beta$ are the roots of $x^2-q x+r=0$

$\therefore \quad$ sum of roots $=\mathrm{q}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\alpha}{2}+2 \beta=q \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha+4 \beta=2 q\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Subtracting (i) from (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 3 \beta=2 q-p \\ & \Rightarrow \beta=\frac{2 q-p}{3} \end{aligned}$$

From (i),

$$\begin{aligned} & \alpha+\frac{2 q-p}{3}=p \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{2(2 p-q)}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Product of roots $=\alpha \beta$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow r=\alpha \beta & =\frac{2(2 p-q)}{3} \times \frac{2 q-p}{3} \\ & =\frac{2}{9}(2 p-q)(2 q-p) \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation $(\operatorname{cosp}-1) x^2+(\operatorname{cosp}) x+\sin p=0$ in the variable $x$, has real roots. Then p can take any value in the interval
explanation:
Given equation is

$$(\cos \mathrm{p}-1) x^2+(\cos \mathrm{p}) x+\sin \mathrm{p}=0$$

Comparing with $a x^2+b x+c=0$, we get

$$a=\cos p-1, b=\cos p, c=\sin p$$

It has real roots.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & b^2-4 a c \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 p-4(\cos p-1)(\sin p) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 p-4 \sin p \cos p+4 \sin p \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 p-4 \sin p \cos p+4 \sin ^2 p \\ \quad & +4 \sin p-4 \sin ^2 p \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\cos p-2 \sin p)^2+4 \sin p(1-\sin p) \geq 0 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad(\cos \mathrm{p}-2 \sin \mathrm{p})$ is always positive

$\therefore \quad 1-\sin p \geq 0$ for all values of $p, p \in(0, \pi)$

content: The equation $(\operatorname{cosp}-1) x^2+(\cos p) x+\operatorname{sinp}=0$ in the variable $x$, has real roots. Then p can take any value in the interval
explanation:
Given equation is

$$(\cos \mathrm{p}-1) x^2+(\cos \mathrm{p}) x+\sin \mathrm{p}=0$$

Comparing with $a x^2+b x+c=0$, we get

$$a=\cos p-1, b=\cos p, c=\sin p$$

It has real roots.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & b^2-4 a \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 p-4(\cos p-1)(\sin p) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 p-4 \sin p \cos p+4 \sin p \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 p-4 \sin p \cos p+4 \sin ^2 p \\ & \quad+4 \sin p-4 \sin ^2 p \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\cos p-2 \sin p)^2+4 \sin p(1-\sin p) \geq 0 \\ \therefore \quad & (\cos p-2 \sin p) \text { is always positive } \\ \therefore \quad & 1-\sin p \geq 0 \text { for all values of } p, p \in(0, \pi) \end{array}$$

Let $$\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3+x-1$$

A root of $$\mathrm{f}(x)$$ exists, if $$\mathrm{f}(x)=0$$ for at least one value of $$x$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(0)=-1<0 \\ & \mathrm{f}(1)=1>0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ By intermediate value theorem, there has to be a point '$$c$$' between 0 and 1 such that $$\mathrm{f}(x)=0$$.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The given equation has exactly one real root.

Alternate Method:

Let $$\mathrm{f}(x)=x^3+x-1$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2+1$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)>0 \quad \forall x \in \mathrm{R}$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)$$ is an increasing function.

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)$$ intersects $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis at only one point.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The given equation has exactly one real root.

content: The quadratic equation whose roots are the numbers having arithmetic mean 34 and geometric mean 16 is
explanation:
To find the quadratic equation with roots having an arithmetic mean of 34 and a geometric mean of 16, let's denote the roots by $a$ and $b$.

The arithmetic mean of the roots is given by:

$$ \frac{a + b}{2} = 34 $$

which implies:

$$ a + b = 68 $$

The geometric mean of the roots is given by:

$$ \sqrt{ab} = 16 $$

which implies:

$$ ab = 16^2 = 256 $$

A quadratic equation with roots $a$ and $b$ can be expressed as:

$$ x^2 - (a+b)x + ab = 0 $$

Substituting the values of $a + b$ and $ab$ into the equation, we have:

$$ x^2 - 68x + 256 = 0 $$

Therefore, the quadratic equation is:

Option C:

$ x^2 - 68x + 256 = 0 $

content: The rational form of a number 1.$\overline{41}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } x=1.414141 \quad \text{... (i)}\\ & 100 x=141.414141\quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Subtract Eq. (i) from Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} 99 x & =140 \\ x & =\frac{140}{99} \\ \therefore 1. \overline{41} & =\frac{140}{99} \end{aligned}$$

We have, $A=\left\{x \in R\left|x^2+5\right| x \mid+6=0\right\}$

Now, $x^2+5|x|+6=0\quad\text{.... (i)}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x^2+3|x|+2|x|+6=0 \\ & \Rightarrow|x|(|x|+3)+2(|x|+3 \mid)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(|x|+2)(|x|+3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow|x|=-2 \text { or }|x|=-3 \\ & \Rightarrow x \neq 2 \text { or } x \neq 3 \end{aligned}$$

Here, no value of $x$ satisfy the Eq. (i).

$$\therefore \quad n(A)=0$$
